gf.title: GitFlow Visualizer
gfv.request.environment.promote: Full Promotion: develop → qa → staging → production
gfv.request.environment.hotfix: Production Hotfix: emergency fix on production
gfv.request.environment.rollback: Rollback: revert bad deployment on production
classic.name: Classic GitFlow
classic.modes.feature.label: Feature Flow
classic.modes.feature.desc: A feature branch is created from develop, work is done in isolation, then merged back into develop via pull request. Feature branches never touch main directly.
classic.modes.release.label: Release Flow
classic.modes.release.desc: A release branch is cut from develop for stabilization. Bug fixes go into the release branch, which is then merged into both main (with a version tag) and back into develop.
classic.modes.hotfix.label: Hotfix Flow
classic.modes.hotfix.desc: A hotfix branch is created directly from main to address a critical production bug. After the fix, it is merged into both main (with a patch tag) and develop.
classic.details.feature.principles: Strict branch hierarchy: main > develop > feature branches
Develop is the integration branch where all features converge
Feature branches are short-lived and isolated from other work
Merges to develop happen via pull request with code review
Feature branches never merge directly into main

classic.details.feature.concepts.definition: Production-ready branch. Only receives merges from release and hotfix branches. Every commit on main is a release.
Integration branch for the next release. All feature branches are merged here. Serves as the base for release branches.
Short-lived branch for isolated feature development. Created from develop, merged back into develop when complete.

classic.details.feature.tradeoffs.pros: Clear structure with well-defined branch purposes
Code review enforced through pull requests
Easy to track what is in progress vs. completed
classic.details.feature.tradeoffs.cons: Can be complex for small teams or simple projects
Merge conflicts accumulate if feature branches live too long
Overhead of branch management for minor changes
classic.details.feature.tradeoffs.whenTo: Teams with multiple developers working on features in parallel, projects that require code review before integration, and codebases where isolation of in-progress work is important.
classic.details.release.principles: Release branches allow stabilization without blocking new feature work on develop
Only bug fixes and release preparation go into a release branch
Release branch merges into main for production and back into develop to preserve fixes
Version tags on main mark each production release
No new features are added to a release branch after it is cut

classic.details.release.concepts.definition: Stabilization branch cut from develop. Receives only bug fixes and version bumps. Merged into main and develop when ready.
A git tag (e.g., v1.0) applied to main after a release merge. Marks the exact commit deployed to production.
After merging release into main, the release branch is also merged back into develop to ensure fixes are not lost.

classic.details.release.tradeoffs.pros: Develop remains open for new features while release stabilizes
Clear separation between feature work and release preparation
Version history is explicit through tags on main
Bug fixes during stabilization propagate to both main and develop
classic.details.release.tradeoffs.cons: Two merges required for every release (main + develop)
Merge conflicts possible when merging back to develop
Extra branch management overhead for frequent releases
classic.details.release.tradeoffs.whenTo: Projects with scheduled release cycles, teams that need a stabilization period before production, and systems where release preparation (version bumps, changelog) is a distinct phase.
classic.details.hotfix.principles: Hotfix branches are created directly from main to fix critical production issues
Hotfixes bypass the normal develop-release cycle for speed
After fixing, hotfix merges into both main (with a patch tag) and develop
Hotfix branches are deleted immediately after merging
Only emergency fixes go through the hotfix flow — not planned work

classic.details.hotfix.concepts.definition: Emergency branch created from main. Contains only the critical fix. Merged into main and develop, then deleted.
A version tag (e.g., v1.0.1) applied to main after a hotfix merge. Indicates a patch release.
Hotfix merges into both main (for immediate deployment) and develop (so the fix is included in future releases).

classic.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.pros: Fast path to production for critical fixes
Does not disrupt ongoing feature or release work
Fix automatically propagates to develop branch
Clear audit trail with patch version tags
classic.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.cons: Risk of conflicts when merging hotfix back into develop
Can be abused for non-emergency changes, eroding process discipline
Requires discipline to keep hotfix scope minimal
classic.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.whenToU: Critical production bugs that cannot wait for the next release cycle. Security vulnerabilities, data corruption issues, or service outages requiring immediate patch deployment.
github.name: GitHub Flow
github.modes.feature.label: Feature + PR
github.modes.feature.desc: Branch from main, open a Pull Request, merge and deploy — the standard GitHub Flow cycle
github.modes.deploy.label: Deploy from Main
github.modes.deploy.desc: Merge to main triggers CI/CD pipeline — tests run, build completes, auto-deploy to production
github.modes.hotfix.label: Quick Fix
github.modes.hotfix.desc: Create a short-lived fix branch, patch the issue via PR, merge and deploy immediately
github.details.feature.principles: Branch off main for every piece of work
Open a Pull Request for code review before merging

github.details.feature.concepts.definition: Single long-lived branch, always in a deployable state
Short-lived branches created from main for each task
Code review gate before merging into main
Every merge to main triggers an automatic deploy

github.details.feature.tradeoffs.pros: CI/CD friendly — deploy on every merge
github.details.feature.tradeoffs.cons: No dedicated staging or release branches
Risky for complex releases requiring coordination
Rollback relies on reverting commits or re-deploying
github.details.feature.tradeoffs.whenToU: Small teams, web applications, SaaS products, and projects practicing continuous deployment where main should always be production-ready.
github.details.deploy.principles: Every merge to main triggers an automatic deploy to production
No staging branch gate — CI/CD pipeline is the quality gate
Tests, builds, and deploys are automated pipeline steps, not manual processes
Tag releases after successful production deployment

github.details.deploy.concepts.definition: Single long-lived branch, always in a deployable state
Automated gate that runs tests, builds, and deploys on every merge to main
Production deployment triggered automatically — no manual promotion or staging branch
Git tags mark specific deployments as versioned releases

github.details.deploy.tradeoffs.pros: Zero manual deployment steps — fully automated pipeline
Fast feedback loop — code reaches production within minutes
No environment branch drift — main is always production
Simple rollback via revert commit + automatic redeploy
github.details.deploy.tradeoffs.cons: Requires robust test suite — no staging safety net
Feature flags needed for partially-complete work
github.details.deploy.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Teams with strong CI/CD culture, comprehensive automated tests, and applications where every merge should go live immediately without manual gates.
github.details.hotfix.principles: Branch off main for every piece of work
Open a Pull Request for code review before merging

github.details.hotfix.concepts.definition: Single long-lived branch, always in a deployable state
Short-lived branches created from main for each task
Code review gate before merging into main
Every merge to main triggers an automatic deploy

github.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.pros: CI/CD friendly — deploy on every merge
github.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.cons: No dedicated staging or release branches
Risky for complex releases requiring coordination
Rollback relies on reverting commits or re-deploying
github.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Small teams, web applications, SaaS products, and projects practicing continuous deployment where main should always be production-ready.
gitlab.name: GitLab Flow
gitlab.modes.feature.label: Feature to Main
gitlab.modes.feature.desc: Feature branch is created from main, developed, reviewed via merge request, and merged back. The feature branch is deleted after merge.
gitlab.modes.staging.label: Promote to Staging
gitlab.modes.staging.desc: Commits accumulated on main are promoted to the staging environment branch for integration testing before production.
gitlab.modes.production.label: Promote to Production
gitlab.modes.production.desc: After staging validation, the staging branch is promoted to the production branch, tagged, and deployed to the live environment.
gitlab.details.feature.principles: Upstream first — fixes always land on main, then promote downstream
Environment branches represent deployment targets, not development stages
Merge only downstream: main → staging → production

gitlab.details.feature.concepts.definition: Primary development branch; all features merge here first
Pre-production environment branch for integration testing
Live environment branch; represents what is deployed to users
Bugs are fixed on main and promoted down — never patch production directly

gitlab.details.feature.tradeoffs.pros: Clear environment tracking — each branch mirrors a deployment target
Simple promotion path: main → staging → production
Upstream first policy prevents drift between environments
gitlab.details.feature.tradeoffs.cons: Merge overhead when promoting through multiple environments
Environment branches can diverge if promotion is delayed
gitlab.details.feature.tradeoffs.whenToU: Teams that maintain multiple deployment environments (staging, QA, production) and need a clear, auditable promotion path with staged deployments.
gitlab.details.staging.principles: Upstream first — fixes always land on main, then promote downstream
Environment branches represent deployment targets, not development stages
Merge only downstream: main → staging → production

gitlab.details.staging.concepts.definition: Primary development branch; all features merge here first
Pre-production environment branch for integration testing
Live environment branch; represents what is deployed to users
Bugs are fixed on main and promoted down — never patch production directly

gitlab.details.staging.tradeoffs.pros: Clear environment tracking — each branch mirrors a deployment target
Simple promotion path: main → staging → production
Upstream first policy prevents drift between environments
gitlab.details.staging.tradeoffs.cons: Merge overhead when promoting through multiple environments
Environment branches can diverge if promotion is delayed
gitlab.details.staging.tradeoffs.whenToU: Teams that maintain multiple deployment environments (staging, QA, production) and need a clear, auditable promotion path with staged deployments.
gitlab.details.production.principles: Upstream first — fixes always land on main, then promote downstream
Environment branches represent deployment targets, not development stages
Merge only downstream: main → staging → production

gitlab.details.production.concepts.definition: Primary development branch; all features merge here first
Pre-production environment branch for integration testing
Live environment branch; represents what is deployed to users
Bugs are fixed on main and promoted down — never patch production directly

gitlab.details.production.tradeoffs.pros: Clear environment tracking — each branch mirrors a deployment target
Simple promotion path: main → staging → production
Upstream first policy prevents drift between environments
gitlab.details.production.tradeoffs.cons: Merge overhead when promoting through multiple environments
Environment branches can diverge if promotion is delayed
gitlab.details.production.tradeoffs.when: Teams that maintain multiple deployment environments (staging, QA, production) and need a clear, auditable promotion path with staged deployments.
trunk.name: Trunk-Based Development
trunk.modes.direct.label: Direct to Trunk
trunk.modes.direct.desc: All developers commit directly to main. No branches, no PRs — just frequent, small commits with CI running on every push. Deploys happen continuously from trunk.
trunk.modes.short-lived.label: Short-lived Branch
trunk.modes.short-lived.desc: A short-lived feature branch is created, worked on for a few hours, merged back to main quickly, and deleted. Branch lifetime must stay under one day.
trunk.modes.flags.label: Feature Flags
trunk.modes.flags.desc: Incomplete features are committed directly to trunk behind feature flags. Flags control gradual rollout: 0% → 10% → 100%. Once fully enabled, the flag code is removed.
trunk.details.direct.principles: Branches live less than one day

trunk.details.direct.concepts.definition: Single source of truth; all code flows through main
Hours, not days; merge early and often
Decouple deployment from release; toggle features at runtime
Commit frequently, run tests on every push

trunk.details.direct.tradeoffs.pros: Minimal merge conflicts due to frequent integration
Fast CI feedback — every commit is tested immediately
Simple branching model — easy to understand and maintain
trunk.details.direct.tradeoffs.cons: Requires strong discipline and small, atomic commits
Feature flags add complexity to codebase and configuration
Risky without comprehensive test coverage and CI pipeline
trunk.details.direct.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Experienced teams with strong CI/CD pipelines, high trust, and fast automated tests. Used at Google, Facebook, and other large-scale engineering organizations.
trunk.details.short-lived.principles: Branches live less than one day

trunk.details.short-lived.concepts.definition: Single source of truth; all code flows through main
Hours, not days; merge early and often
Decouple deployment from release; toggle features at runtime
Commit frequently, run tests on every push

trunk.details.short-lived.tradeoffs.pros: Minimal merge conflicts due to frequent integration
Fast CI feedback — every commit is tested immediately
Simple branching model — easy to understand and maintain
trunk.details.short-lived.tradeoffs.cons: Requires strong discipline and small, atomic commits
Feature flags add complexity to codebase and configuration
Risky without comprehensive test coverage and CI pipeline
trunk.details.short-lived.tradeoffs.when: Experienced teams with strong CI/CD pipelines, high trust, and fast automated tests. Used at Google, Facebook, and other large-scale engineering organizations.
trunk.details.flags.principles: Branches live less than one day

trunk.details.flags.concepts.definition: Single source of truth; all code flows through main
Hours, not days; merge early and often
Decouple deployment from release; toggle features at runtime
Commit frequently, run tests on every push

trunk.details.flags.tradeoffs.pros: Minimal merge conflicts due to frequent integration
Fast CI feedback — every commit is tested immediately
Simple branching model — easy to understand and maintain
trunk.details.flags.tradeoffs.cons: Requires strong discipline and small, atomic commits
Feature flags add complexity to codebase and configuration
Risky without comprehensive test coverage and CI pipeline
trunk.details.flags.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Experienced teams with strong CI/CD pipelines, high trust, and fast automated tests. Used at Google, Facebook, and other large-scale engineering organizations.
oneflow.name: OneFlow
oneflow.modes.release.label: Release via Rebase
oneflow.modes.release.desc: A release branch is created from main, stabilized with fixes, then rebased onto main and fast-forward merged to preserve a linear history. The release branch is deleted after tagging.
oneflow.modes.hotfix.label: Hotfix Cherry-pick
oneflow.modes.hotfix.desc: A hotfix is committed on main and cherry-picked to the active release branch. This allows targeted backporting of critical fixes without merging unrelated changes.
oneflow.modes.feature.label: Feature Rebase
oneflow.modes.feature.desc: A feature branch is created from main, developed in isolation, then rebased onto main and fast-forward merged. This keeps the commit history linear and avoids merge commits.
oneflow.details.release.principles: Single main branch — the only long-lived branch
No develop branch — simplicity over ceremony
Rebase instead of merge commits — keep history linear
Release branches are short-lived stabilization branches
Linear history is the primary goal

oneflow.details.release.concepts.definition: The only long-lived branch; always deployable
Short-lived branch for release stabilization; rebased onto main before fast-forward merge
Replay commits on top of another branch tip to maintain linear history
Moves branch pointer forward without a merge commit — preserves linear history

oneflow.details.release.tradeoffs.pros: Simpler than Classic GitFlow — fewer long-lived branches
Fewer branches to manage and reason about
oneflow.details.release.tradeoffs.cons: Force-push needed after rebase on shared branches
Harder for beginners compared to merge-based flows
oneflow.details.release.tradeoffs.whenTo: Teams wanting a clean, linear history with a single deployable branch and the discipline to rebase consistently.
oneflow.details.hotfix.principles: Single main branch — the only long-lived branch
Hotfixes are committed on main and cherry-picked to release branches
Cherry-pick allows selective backporting without full merges
Release branches receive only targeted fixes
Linear history is the primary goal

oneflow.details.hotfix.concepts.definition: The only long-lived branch; all new work lands here first
Selective backport of individual commits between branches without merging entire histories
Stabilization branch that receives cherry-picked hotfixes from main
A git tag marking a patch release on the release branch after a hotfix

oneflow.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.pros: Hotfixes land on main first — single source of truth
Cherry-pick avoids pulling unrelated changes into release
Patch releases are straightforward and isolated
oneflow.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.cons: Cherry-pick can cause conflicts if commits depend on other changes
Requires careful tracking of which fixes have been backported
Manual cherry-pick process does not scale well with many release branches
oneflow.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.whenToU: Teams maintaining one or more release branches that need targeted hotfixes without merging all recent main development.
oneflow.details.feature.principles: Single main branch — the only long-lived branch
No develop branch — simplicity over ceremony
Feature branches rebase onto main before fast-forward merge
Rebase instead of merge commits — keep history linear
Linear history is the primary goal

oneflow.details.feature.concepts.definition: The only long-lived branch; always deployable
Short-lived branch for isolated feature development; rebased onto main when complete
Replay commits on top of another branch tip to maintain linear history
Moves branch pointer forward without a merge commit — preserves linear history

oneflow.details.feature.tradeoffs.pros: Clean, linear commit history with no merge commits
Simple mental model — only main and short-lived feature branches
Easy to bisect and review history
oneflow.details.feature.tradeoffs.cons: Force-push needed after rebase if feature branch was shared
Harder for beginners compared to merge-based flows
oneflow.details.feature.tradeoffs.whenTo: Small to medium teams that value a linear commit history and are comfortable with interactive rebase workflows.
environment.name: Environment Branching
environment.modes.promote.label: Full Promotion
environment.modes.promote.desc: Code promotes forward through environment branches: develop to qa to staging to production. Each environment has its own long-lived branch. Deployments happen when code is merged into an environment branch.
environment.modes.hotfix.label: Production Hotfix
environment.modes.hotfix.desc: An emergency fix is committed directly to the production branch, deployed immediately, then synced back to develop so future promotions include the fix.
environment.modes.rollback.label: Rollback
environment.modes.rollback.desc: A bad deployment to production is reverted by applying a revert commit on the production branch, redeploying the previous known-good state, and tagging the rollback.
environment.details.promote.principles: Each environment has its own long-lived branch
Code promotes forward only: develop → qa → staging → production
All changes enter through develop — never commit directly to environment branches
Never merge backward (except hotfix sync from production to develop)
Deploying means merging into the target environment branch
Every promotion is an auditable merge commit

environment.details.promote.concepts.definition: Active development branch where new features land. Starting point of the promotion pipeline. All fixes and changes must be committed here first.
Quality assurance branch. Receives merges from develop only — never direct commits. If a QA issue is found, the fix goes through develop and is re-promoted.
Pre-production environment. Receives merges from qa only — never direct commits. Config changes must flow from develop through the pipeline.
Live environment branch. Only receives merges from staging (or hotfix commits in emergencies).
Forward merge from one environment branch to the next. The mechanism by which code advances toward production.

environment.details.promote.tradeoffs.pr: Clear mapping between branches and environments
Full audit trail of what was deployed and when
Each environment can be inspected independently
environment.details.promote.tradeoffs.co: Complex merges as environments drift apart
Risk of environment drift if promotions are infrequent
environment.details.promote.tradeoffs.wh: Regulated industries, organizations with multiple deployment environments, teams that require compliance and audit trails for every promotion.
environment.details.hotfix.principles: Emergency fixes go directly on the production branch
Hotfix is deployed immediately after commit
Production is synced back to develop to propagate the fix
Hotfix sync is the only allowed backward merge
Patch tags mark every hotfix deployment

environment.details.hotfix.concepts.definition: Live branch that receives the emergency fix directly, bypassing the normal promotion pipeline.
Receives a sync merge from production after the hotfix to keep future promotions consistent.
A backward merge from production to develop. The only exception to the forward-only rule.

environment.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.pro: Fastest path to fix a production issue
Fix is immediately reflected in the live branch
Sync merge ensures develop stays consistent
environment.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.con: Risk of introducing regressions without full testing
Backward merge can cause conflicts on develop
Requires discipline to limit scope to true emergencies
environment.details.hotfix.tradeoffs.whe: Critical production outages, security vulnerabilities, or data-integrity issues that cannot wait for a full promotion cycle.
environment.details.rollback.principles: Rollback is a revert commit on the production branch, not a branch reset
The bad deployment is recorded in history, then undone
A new deployment restores the previous known-good state
Rollback tags mark the recovery point
No history rewriting — the audit trail is preserved

environment.details.rollback.concepts.definition: Source of the bad merge that was promoted to production.
Live branch where the revert commit is applied to undo the bad deployment.
A commit that undoes the changes of a previous merge. Preserves full history while restoring the prior state.

environment.details.rollback.tradeoffs.p: Full audit trail of the incident and rollback
Production branch remains linear and clean
Easy to re-promote once the issue is fixed
environment.details.rollback.tradeoffs.c: Revert commit adds noise to the git log
Re-promoting the same code later requires reverting the revert
Does not address the root cause — only restores state
Staging and qa branches may still contain the bad code
environment.details.rollback.tradeoffs.w: Failed deployments that cause user-facing issues, performance regressions detected after go-live, or any situation requiring immediate rollback to a known-good state.
release.name: Release Branching
release.modes.create.label: Create Release
release.modes.create.desc: Develop on main, branch release/1.0 for stabilization, fix bugs, merge back, tag, and clean up. The release branch isolates stabilization from ongoing feature work.
release.modes.patch.label: Patch Release
release.modes.patch.desc: Start from a tagged release, create a release branch for a targeted patch fix, merge back into main, tag the patch version, and clean up.
release.modes.parallel.label: Parallel Releases
release.modes.parallel.desc: Maintain two release branches simultaneously from main. Each release stabilizes independently, merges and tags on its own schedule.
release.details.create.principles: Release branches isolate stabilization work from ongoing development
main continues to receive new features while a release stabilizes
Bug fixes during stabilization go to the release branch, not directly to main
After merge, the release branch is deleted to keep the repository clean

release.details.create.concepts.definition: A branch created from main to stabilize a specific version before shipping. Only bug fixes and stabilization commits are allowed.
The process of hardening a release candidate by fixing bugs and running final QA, without new feature work.
A git tag (e.g., v1.0) applied to main after the release branch is merged. Marks the exact production-ready commit.
After release, the release branch is merged into main so that stabilization fixes are included in future development.

release.details.create.tradeoffs.pros: Isolates stabilization without blocking new development on main
Clear release lifecycle: branch, stabilize, tag, delete
Easy to identify which commits belong to a specific release
release.details.create.tradeoffs.cons: Merge conflicts can occur when merging release back into main
Requires discipline to avoid adding features to the release branch
Short-lived but adds branching overhead compared to trunk-based development
release.details.create.tradeoffs.whenToU: Projects that ship distinct releases with a stabilization phase — mobile apps, libraries, desktop software, and any team that needs a dedicated pre-release hardening period.
release.details.patch.principles: Patches are applied to a release branch created from the tagged version
Only targeted bug fixes go into the patch — no new features
The patch is merged back into main and tagged with a new patch version (e.g., v1.0.1)
The release branch is deleted after the patch is merged and tagged

release.details.patch.concepts.definition: A bug fix applied to an existing release after the initial version has shipped. Results in a new patch version tag.
Selectively applying specific commits from one branch to another, often used to port fixes between release branches.
A version tag (e.g., v1.0.1) applied after a patch fix. Follows semantic versioning for the patch segment.
A branch recreated from the original release tag specifically to apply and test a patch fix in isolation.

release.details.patch.tradeoffs.pros: Targeted fixes without disrupting ongoing development
Clear audit trail of what changed in each patch
Semantic versioning makes patch releases easy to track
Minimal risk — only the specific fix is applied
release.details.patch.tradeoffs.cons: Requires careful cherry-picking or selective merging
Older releases may diverge significantly from current main
Multiple patches can create complex merge histories
release.details.patch.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Any versioned software that needs to ship urgent bug fixes for already-released versions — security patches, critical bug fixes, compliance updates.
release.details.parallel.principles: Multiple release branches can coexist for parallel version support
Each release branch stabilizes and ships independently
Main continues to receive new features while releases are maintained
Cross-merging between release branches is not allowed — fixes flow through main

release.details.parallel.concepts.definition: Multiple release branches maintained simultaneously for different versions, each with its own stabilization and release cycle.
Each release branch is isolated from others, ensuring changes in one version do not affect another.
Each release branch merges and tags independently — v1.0 and v2.0 can ship on different schedules.
A release branch kept alive for extended maintenance, receiving only critical fixes while newer versions are developed.

release.details.parallel.tradeoffs.pros: Supports parallel version maintenance for different customers or platforms
Each release has its own stabilization timeline
Well-suited for libraries and frameworks with multiple supported versions
Clear separation between major version lines
release.details.parallel.tradeoffs.cons: Increased merge complexity with multiple long-lived branches
Risk of divergent branches over time
Requires discipline and tooling to keep releases in sync with main
Higher maintenance burden for the team
release.details.parallel.tradeoffs.whenT: Libraries, frameworks, and enterprise software that must maintain multiple major versions simultaneously — e.g., supporting v1.x and v2.x customers in parallel.
forking.name: Forking Workflow
forking.modes.contribute.label: Fork + PR
forking.modes.contribute.desc: Fork upstream, sync origin, create feature branch, commit, open PR to upstream, merge, sync back, clean up.
forking.modes.sync.label: Sync Fork
forking.modes.sync.desc: Upstream advances while fork is outdated, then contributor syncs fork with upstream.
forking.modes.review.label: Maintainer Review
forking.modes.review.desc: Contributor opens PR from fork, maintainer reviews with comments, contributor updates, maintainer merges, contributor syncs.
forking.details.contribute.principles: Contributors don't have write access to the upstream repository
All changes are proposed via fork + Pull Request
Maintainers review and merge contributions into upstream
Contributors must keep their fork in sync with upstream

forking.details.contribute.concepts.definition: The original repository owned by the project maintainers
The contributor's personal fork (full copy of the repo)
A complete copy of a repository under a different owner
A cross-repo merge request from fork to upstream
Fetching upstream changes into the fork to stay up to date

forking.details.contribute.tradeoffs.pro: No write access needed to the upstream repo
Safe for open-source — contributors work in isolation
Clear ownership and authorship of changes
forking.details.contribute.tradeoffs.con: Sync overhead — fork can drift out of date
More complex workflow compared to shared-repo models
forking.details.contribute.tradeoffs.whe: Open-source projects, external contributors, environments with untrusted contributors where upstream write access must be restricted.
forking.details.sync.principles: Forks must be regularly synced with upstream to avoid drift
Use git fetch upstream + merge or rebase to incorporate changes
Resolve conflicts locally before pushing to origin
Sync before starting any new feature branch

forking.details.sync.concepts.definition: A git remote pointing to the original repository
Download commits from upstream without merging them
Integrate upstream changes into the local fork branch
Divergence between fork and upstream over time

forking.details.sync.tradeoffs.pros: Keeps fork up to date and reduces merge conflicts
Ensures feature branches start from latest upstream state
forking.details.sync.tradeoffs.cons: Requires manual effort to sync regularly
Merge conflicts may arise during sync
forking.details.sync.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Whenever contributing to an active upstream repository where new commits land frequently.
forking.details.review.principles: Maintainers gate all changes via code review before merging
Contributors address review feedback by pushing new commits to the PR
PRs are the single entry point for changes into upstream
After merge, contributors sync their fork to stay current

forking.details.review.concepts.definition: Maintainer inspection of proposed changes before merge
Feedback left on specific lines or overall PR by reviewers
Pushing additional commits to an open PR to address feedback
Maintainer sign-off indicating the PR is ready to merge

forking.details.review.tradeoffs.pros: Ensures code quality through maintainer oversight
Knowledge sharing between contributor and maintainer
Catches bugs and design issues before they reach upstream
forking.details.review.tradeoffs.cons: Review turnaround can slow down contribution velocity
Back-and-forth cycles can be frustrating for contributors
forking.details.review.tradeoffs.whenToU: All open-source projects and any team where code quality gates and maintainer approval are required before merging.
ship-show-ask.name: Ship / Show / Ask
ship-show-ask.modes.ship.label: Ship (Direct Merge)
ship-show-ask.modes.ship.desc: Low-risk change: branch from main, commit fix, merge directly (no PR), deploy.
ship-show-ask.modes.show.label: Show (PR, No Block)
ship-show-ask.modes.show.desc: Medium-risk change: branch from main, work on feature, open non-blocking PR for visibility, merge, deploy.
ship-show-ask.modes.ask.label: Ask (PR + Review)
ship-show-ask.modes.ask.desc: High-risk change: branch from main, work on feature, open blocking PR, address review, merge, deploy.
ship-show-ask.details.ship.principles: Low-risk, trivial changes merged directly to main
No PR required — trust the developer's judgment
Fastest path to production for safe changes

ship-show-ask.details.ship.concepts.definition: Trivial or low-risk changes merged directly to main without a PR
Push or merge to main without opening a pull request
Engineers classify their own changes; the team trusts individual judgment

ship-show-ask.details.ship.tradeoffs.pro: Fastest delivery — no review overhead for trivial changes
Reduces bottlenecks and keeps velocity high
ship-show-ask.details.ship.tradeoffs.con: No review safety net for shipped changes
Risky if a change is misclassified as low-risk
ship-show-ask.details.ship.tradeoffs.whe: Trivial changes like typo fixes, config tweaks, or minor documentation updates where the risk of breakage is negligible.
ship-show-ask.details.show.principles: Medium-risk changes opened as a non-blocking PR for team visibility
Merge proceeds without waiting for review approval
Team stays informed without slowing down delivery

ship-show-ask.details.show.concepts.definition: Informational PR opened for visibility; merge proceeds without blocking on review
A pull request that serves as notification, not a gate — merge is allowed without approval
Keeping the team aware of changes without requiring formal sign-off

ship-show-ask.details.show.tradeoffs.pro: Creates a record of changes for future reference
Encourages knowledge sharing across the team
ship-show-ask.details.show.tradeoffs.con: Reviews may never happen if not prioritized
Issues discovered post-merge require follow-up work
Requires discipline to actually review "show" PRs
ship-show-ask.details.show.tradeoffs.whe: Medium-risk refactors, internal module changes, or improvements where team awareness is valuable but blocking review would slow delivery unnecessarily.
ship-show-ask.details.ask.principles: High-risk or complex changes require a blocking PR with review
Merge is gated on explicit approval from reviewers
Ensures quality and shared understanding for critical changes

ship-show-ask.details.ask.concepts.definition: PR with a blocking review required before the change can be merged
Merge is not permitted until one or more reviewers approve the change
Reviewers may request changes that must be addressed before approval

ship-show-ask.details.ask.tradeoffs.pros: Catches issues before they reach production
Builds shared understanding of complex changes
Provides a strong audit trail for critical work
ship-show-ask.details.ask.tradeoffs.cons: Slower delivery due to review wait times
Can become a bottleneck if reviewers are unavailable
ship-show-ask.details.ask.tradeoffs.when: High-risk changes like authentication rewrites, database migrations, API contract changes, or any work that could significantly impact users or system stability.
scaled.name: Scaled GitFlow
scaled.modes.team.label: Team Branch Flow
scaled.modes.team.desc: A team branch is created from develop for isolated team-level work. Multiple commits happen on the team branch, then it is merged back into develop. Develop is eventually merged into main and tagged for release.
scaled.modes.program.label: Program Integration
scaled.modes.program.desc: Multiple team branches are created from develop for parallel team work. Each team merges into develop independently. Once all teams have integrated, develop is merged into main, tagged, and deployed as a program increment.
scaled.modes.release.label: Scaled Release
scaled.modes.release.desc: A release branch (program increment) is cut from develop for stabilization. Bug fixes go into the release branch, which is then merged into main with a version tag and back into develop to preserve fixes.
scaled.details.team.principles: Team branches provide isolation for team-level work without affecting other teams
Program increment integration happens through the shared develop branch
Release trains coordinate delivery across multiple teams on a schedule
Multiple teams contribute to a shared develop branch as the integration point

scaled.details.team.concepts.definition: Team-level isolation branch. Created from develop, used for a single team's work during a program increment. Merged back into develop when complete.
A scheduled integration cycle (typically 8-12 weeks) where all teams plan, develop, and integrate their work into a shared codebase.
A coordinated release mechanism that aligns multiple teams to deliver on a fixed cadence, regardless of individual team completion.
The integration point where all team branches converge. Serves as the source of truth for the next release.

scaled.details.team.tradeoffs.pros: Scales to large organizations with many teams
Clear integration points reduce last-minute surprises
scaled.details.team.tradeoffs.cons: High complexity in branch management and merge coordination
Merge conflicts escalate when many teams integrate simultaneously
Requires significant tooling and process discipline
scaled.details.team.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Large enterprises adopting SAFe or scaled agile frameworks, organizations with 5+ teams contributing to the same product, and systems requiring coordinated release trains.
scaled.details.program.principles: Team branches provide isolation for team-level work without affecting other teams
Program increment integration happens through the shared develop branch
Release trains coordinate delivery across multiple teams on a schedule
Multiple teams contribute to a shared develop branch as the integration point

scaled.details.program.concepts.definition: Team-level isolation branch. Created from develop, used for a single team's work during a program increment. Merged back into develop when complete.
A scheduled integration cycle (typically 8-12 weeks) where all teams plan, develop, and integrate their work into a shared codebase.
A coordinated release mechanism that aligns multiple teams to deliver on a fixed cadence, regardless of individual team completion.
The integration point where all team branches converge. Serves as the source of truth for the next release.

scaled.details.program.tradeoffs.pros: Scales to large organizations with many teams
Clear integration points reduce last-minute surprises
scaled.details.program.tradeoffs.cons: High complexity in branch management and merge coordination
Merge conflicts escalate when many teams integrate simultaneously
Requires significant tooling and process discipline
scaled.details.program.tradeoffs.whenToU: Large enterprises adopting SAFe or scaled agile frameworks, organizations with 5+ teams contributing to the same product, and systems requiring coordinated release trains.
scaled.details.release.principles: Team branches provide isolation for team-level work without affecting other teams
Program increment integration happens through the shared develop branch
Release trains coordinate delivery across multiple teams on a schedule
Multiple teams contribute to a shared develop branch as the integration point

scaled.details.release.concepts.definition: Team-level isolation branch. Created from develop, used for a single team's work during a program increment. Merged back into develop when complete.
A scheduled integration cycle (typically 8-12 weeks) where all teams plan, develop, and integrate their work into a shared codebase.
A coordinated release mechanism that aligns multiple teams to deliver on a fixed cadence, regardless of individual team completion.
The integration point where all team branches converge. Serves as the source of truth for the next release.

scaled.details.release.tradeoffs.pros: Scales to large organizations with many teams
Clear integration points reduce last-minute surprises
scaled.details.release.tradeoffs.cons: High complexity in branch management and merge coordination
Merge conflicts escalate when many teams integrate simultaneously
Requires significant tooling and process discipline
scaled.details.release.tradeoffs.whenToU: Large enterprises adopting SAFe or scaled agile frameworks, organizations with 5+ teams contributing to the same product, and systems requiring coordinated release trains.
cherrypick.name: Cherry-pick Flow
cherrypick.modes.backport.label: Backport Fix
cherrypick.modes.backport.desc: A bug fix is committed on main and cherry-picked to older release branches. Each release receives a patch tag. This keeps all supported versions in sync without merging entire branches.
cherrypick.modes.forward.label: Forward Port
cherrypick.modes.forward.desc: A fix is committed on an older release branch and cherry-picked forward to main. This ensures urgent fixes land in the current release while preserving the fix in the older version.
cherrypick.modes.multi.label: Multi-version
cherrypick.modes.multi.desc: A security fix is committed on main and cherry-picked to all supported release branches. Each release is tagged independently, enabling parallel version maintenance.
cherrypick.details.backport.principles: Fix once in main, cherry-pick to all supported release branches
Backport critical fixes to older versions still in production
Each release branch receives its own patch tag after cherry-pick

cherrypick.details.backport.concepts.definition: Apply a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the entire branch history. Creates a new commit with the same changes.
Porting a fix from a newer version (main) to an older supported release branch. Ensures older versions receive critical patches.
A version tag (e.g., v1.0.1) applied to a release branch after a cherry-picked fix. Marks a point-release for that version.

cherrypick.details.backport.tradeoffs.pr: Targeted fixes with minimal risk to each release
Each version stays isolated — no unintended changes leak across
Clear patch history per release via tags
Fix is authored and tested once on main
cherrypick.details.backport.tradeoffs.co: Easy to miss a cherry-pick to one of several release branches
Cherry-picked commits create divergent history across branches
Manual tracking required to know which fixes reached which releases
cherrypick.details.backport.tradeoffs.wh: Libraries and frameworks with multiple supported versions, long-term support (LTS) releases, and products running different versions in production simultaneously.
cherrypick.details.forward.principles: Fix urgent issues directly on the affected release branch
Forward port the fix to main so it is included in future releases
Tag the release branch immediately for rapid deployment
Keep main up to date with all production fixes

cherrypick.details.forward.concepts.definition: Cherry-picking a fix from an older release branch into main or a newer branch. Ensures the fix is not lost in future versions.
A tag applied to the release branch after the fix, enabling immediate deployment of the patched version.
Applying the release fix commit to main so that the next major version includes the same fix.

cherrypick.details.forward.tradeoffs.pro: Urgent fix lands on the affected version immediately
Main stays synchronized with production fixes
No need to wait for a full release cycle to fix older versions
Version isolation — fix does not pull in unreleased features
cherrypick.details.forward.tradeoffs.con: Risk of forgetting to forward port the fix
Cherry-pick may conflict with newer code on main
Divergent commit history between release and main
cherrypick.details.forward.tradeoffs.whe: When a critical bug is found in a deployed release and the fix must ship immediately, while also ensuring main does not regress in future releases.
cherrypick.details.multi.principles: Fix security issues once on main, propagate to all supported versions
Cherry-pick to every active release branch systematically
Tag each release independently after applying the fix
Maintain a clear record of which versions received the patch

cherrypick.details.multi.concepts.definition: Maintaining multiple release branches in parallel, each receiving critical fixes independently via cherry-pick.
Systematically cherry-picking a security patch from main to all supported release branches to close vulnerabilities across versions.
The set of currently supported release branches. Each must be evaluated for cherry-pick applicability when a fix lands on main.

cherrypick.details.multi.tradeoffs.pros: Each version tagged and deployable independently
Minimal risk — only the specific fix is applied
Clear audit trail of security patches per version
cherrypick.details.multi.tradeoffs.cons: Scales poorly as the number of supported versions grows
Cherry-pick conflicts increase with version divergence
Requires tooling or discipline to track propagation status
cherrypick.details.multi.tradeoffs.whenT: Open-source libraries with multiple active major versions, enterprise software with contractual LTS obligations, and any system where multiple production versions must be patched simultaneously.
